# UC-19.1.4 · Fault Trending by Severity (Cisco UCS)

> **Criticality:** High &middot; **Difficulty:** Intermediate &middot; **Pillar:** Observability &middot; **Type:** Reliability, Availability &middot; **Status:** Verified

*We count how many new problem notices arrive each day from the server farm’s rack-and-blade manager, sorted by how serious each notice is, so the people running the data hall can see if trouble is piling up over weeks—not just what is still open at this hour. That steers long-range repair and upgrade plans so small recurring glitches are handled before they become a year of fire drills.*

---

## Description

Tracks **new** Cisco UCS **faultInst** arrivals (raise-indexed events) over time, normalises **severity** with `coalesce` and `lower()`, omits `cleared` from the daily rate, and uses `bin` plus `stats` so empty severity tiers do not drop a series. Complements UC-19.1.1 state views (`lc!="cleared"`): this measures fault arrival flow and code-family change for reliability work, not a live DN component table for the NOC. Optional panels group by `code`, `type`, or chassis to show which families are accelerating.

## Value

A rising fault arrival rate in **major** and **critical** tiers, or a narrow set of **code** values that persist week over week, gives vendor-escalation evidence, supports problem-management and reliability cadences, and helps prioritise chassis refresh or environmental follow-up before the same nodes keep failing. Short bursts that align to a **maintenance** window differ from a slow code ramp. Use the flow view for programme reporting and calmer (non-pager) anomaly routing; when a code family is suspect, use UC-19.1.1 to inspect current DNs, not a duplicate of the same 24h table with `timechart` on top.

## Implementation

Enable `cisco:ucs:faults` in `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs`, decide deduplication for API+syslog, then run the `spl` on a 30-day window for dashboard panels and a weekly report. For spikes, add a follow-on panel that z-scores daily arrivals per **severity** against a multi-week rolling baseline, route that to a Sev3 or problem-management queue (not a pager), and when a `code` family looks wrong, drill with UC-19.1.1’s active-fault view for the same DNs in the now slice.

## Detailed Implementation

### Prerequisites
- Splunk Add-on for Cisco UCS (`Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs`, Splunkbase 2731, v4.3.1 or newer) on a Heavy Forwarder (or an approved data-collection node) with outbound `443/tcp` HTTPS to each UCS Manager cluster virtual IP, plus the same add-on (or a TA-only bundle) on Search Heads for field discovery, knowledge objects, and saved-search ownership.
- A dedicated UCSM read-only service user whose role can read the `faultInst` class (and, for cross-validation with UC-19.1.1, the equipment orgs that own blades and racks you care about). Do not bind polling to a human’s interactive administrator account. Store the password in the Splunk credential store if the add-on’s modular input supports it; otherwise use host filesystem permissions on `local/inputs.conf`.
- An approved index, typically `cisco_ucs`, and a Splunk role that includes that index in `srchIndexesAllowed`.
- License awareness: the fault stream can be *much* noisier than `cisco:ucs:inventory` because the same `faultInst` can reindex on lifecycle or poll snapshots, and parallel syslog adds volume. Start from one hour of **license usage** and a `_internal` add-on check before you schedule sub-hour artefact collection everywhere.
- Scope clarity: this use case is for classic UCSM-managed **faultInst** in `cisco:ucs:faults`. Intersight-primary compute might never mirror that lifecycle back into the UCSM XML the TA poll reads—govern that in `exclusions`, not in silent assumptions.
- Arrivals vs state (pair with UC-19.1.1): this UC **trends the rate of new fault events** indexed from raise semantics (and deduped for dual feeds). UC-19.1.1 answers “what is **open** and where on the **DN** tree *right now*”. They are **complementary**; building 19.1.4 as a timechart of 19.1.1’s `lc!="cleared"` table would only tell you *how many* open faults *exist* per poll, not a clean *arrival* history, and it would conflate re-seen state with new hardware symptoms.
- Field vocabulary (UCSM): the managed-object class is **faultInst**. **severity** includes `info`, `condition`, `warning`, `minor`, `major`, `critical`, and `cleared`—do not hard-truncate a `fields` list that assumes only a subset, and never let mixed-case strings split chart columns after an upgrade. **code** (for example F0479, F0521, F0408) is a stable *family* label for problem management. **cause** is an enumerated value such as `equipment-degraded` or `link-down`, not a prose field to wildcard with `like`. **type** is a coarse category (`equipment` vs `environmental` vs `network` …) to show whether a fleet-wide increase is a cooling story or a NIC story. **occur** counts retriggers; **created** and **changed** are lifecycle timestamps, with `_time` usually aligned to **created** for raise rows in supported TA builds.
- Retention: keep 30–90 days online for the panels below, longer in a **summary** index for year-over-year programme reviews, with deduped daily aggregates to control licence.

**Step 1 — Configure data collection**
(1) Install `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` on the collection tier and the search tier per the current Splunk and Cisco install guide. In Splunk Web, open **Settings → Data inputs** and look for the Cisco UCS family, or edit `local/inputs.conf` under the app. Copy a documented modular-input template from the shipped `default/inputs.conf` (names usually follow `cisco_ucs://<label>`). Set `disabled = 0`, point the URL to `https://<ucsm-fqdn>`, and bind `index` to your approved `cisco_ucs` (or an override) while preserving the TA’s `cisco:ucs:faults` sourcetype for fault events—do not invent a new sourcetype in `props` unless a Splunk admin owns the follow-on field extractions for every team.
(2) If you also ingest UCSM syslog, route the fault-related lines to the same *logical* sourcetype (or a child you union in searches). Treat dual feeds as a data-architecture choice: the arrival-rate in this UC is wrong until you `dedup` on `dn`, `code`, `severity`, and an agreed `_time` grain (tighten or loosen after you look at *real* skew).
(3) Discovery probes after two poll or syslog minutes: `index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:faults" earliest=-1h | stats count by sourcetype` then, if the field exists, `| stats count by classId` for sanity. For field spellings, run `| head 5 | table _raw, severity, code, type, cause, occur, lc` and compare to a UCSM GUI **Faults** view for a known object.
(4) Optional `ucs_fault_code_catalog.csv` (UTF-8) in a custom app under `lookups/`, for example with columns `code`, `family` (CPU/Memory/PSU/Fan/Network/Storage/Mgmt/Other), `severity_baseline` (narrative, not a second Splunk *severity* field), and `vendor_kb_url` (Cisco’s Faults Reference deep links you already trust). In `transforms.conf` in that same app:
```
[ucs_fault_code_catalog]
filename = ucs_fault_code_catalog.csv
```
Reload the bundle or the deployer for search-head clustering. In dashboards, `| lookup ucs_fault_code_catalog code OUTPUT family, vendor_kb_url` adds human-readable *family* without guessing from `dn` substrings, which 19.1.1 does for live FRU bucketing, not 19.1.4’s **trending** view.

**Step 2 — Create the search and report**
Primary panel — daily arrival rate by `sev` (avoids the old broken `| fields _time critical major minor warning info` pattern):**

```spl
index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:faults" earliest=-30d@d
| dedup dn, code, severity, _time
| eval sev=lower(coalesce(severity, sev, ""))
| eval code=coalesce(code, fault_code, "UNKNOWN")
| eval ftype=lower(coalesce(type, fault_type, ""))
| eval cause=lower(coalesce(cause, fault_cause, ""))
| where sev!="cleared" AND sev!=""
| eval sev_rank=case(sev="critical",1, sev="major",2, sev="minor",3, sev="warning",4, sev="condition",5, sev="info",6, 1=1, 99)
| bin _time span=1d
| stats count as arrivals, dc(dn) as affected_dns, dc(code) as distinct_codes, first(sev_rank) as sev_rank by _time, sev
| sort 0 sev_rank, _time
| chart useother=f sum(arrivals) over _time by sev
```

Why `bin`+`stats`+`chart` instead of a naked `| timechart count by severity` with a manual `| fields`:** any severity tier with zero events in the selected window *does not* become a `timechart` column, and Title-Case severities in older TAs would appear as a parallel set of columns. The explicit `lower` and a single `sev` field prevent that, and the `sev_rank` case keeps a stable stack order in visualisation where you need it. Switch `span=1d` to `span=1h` and `earliest=-7d@d` for a tactical week view; for 90 days of programme history, `span=7d` and `earliest=-90d@d` is often more readable on executive slides.

Companion A — top fault `code` over the last 7 days (paste as a second panel):**

```spl
index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:faults" earliest=-7d@d
| dedup dn, code, severity, _time
| eval sev=lower(coalesce(severity, sev, ""))
| eval code=coalesce(code, fault_code, "UNKNOWN")
| where sev!="cleared" AND sev!=""
| stats count as arrivals by code
| sort 0 - arrivals
| head 20
```

Companion B — rate *anomaly* (example z-score *after* daily stats; tune windows for your change cadence; not a one-line drop-in to every environment):**

```spl
index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:faults" earliest=-90d@d
| dedup dn, code, severity, _time
| eval sev=lower(coalesce(severity, sev, ""))
| where sev!="cleared" AND sev!=""
| bin _time span=1d
| stats count as arrivals by _time, sev
| eventstats avg(arrivals) as base, stdev(arrivals) as base_sd by sev
| eval z=if(base_sd>0, (arrivals-base)/base_sd, 0)
| where z>3
| sort 0 - z
```

Narrow `eventstats` to a *rolling* window with `streamstats` and `window=28` (days) if a single global 90-day mean is too slow to react, or if you have strong seasonality, compute a stricter per **day-of-week** baseline in a `summary` index offline—this is programme tuning, not something to hard-code as “50% over baseline” in an alert with no context.

**Step 3 — Validate against Cisco UCS Manager**
(1) From Companion A, pick the most frequent `code` in the last 7 days. In Splunk, add `| search code="<that-code>"` to the *deduped* base, take **three** `dn` samples, and note whether `occur` is rising on the same `dn` (storm) vs many DNs (fleet).
(2) In UCSM, use **Equipment** to navigate to one of those DNs, open the object’s **Faults** list, and confirm the **code** and lifecycle match what you expect for *that* time window. Remember the GUI’s “active” count is a **state** view; your arrival chart can include faults that are already **cleared** in the *now* column—*that difference is the point of 19.1.4*.
(3) In Cisco’s online **Cisco UCS Faults Reference, Release 4.2** (see `references`), look up the `code` for recommended service actions, affected FRU classes, and whether the fault is a known *transient* during upgrade.
(4) For **dedup** validation, run `| stats count by host, source, sourcetype` on a 2-hour test window. If you still see a stable ~2× pattern after the `dedup` in this UC, the duplicate key set is not `dn+code+severity+_time` for your build—lengthen the time-bucket in `dedup` or extend keys only with evidence, not a guess.

**Step 4 — Operationalise**
(1) Build a “Fault arrival programme” workspace with: the primary stacked chart, Companion A, the heatmap and single-value week-over-week pair described in `visualization`, and (optionally) a `chassis` or org breakdown after you `rex` a `chassis-#` from `dn`—but keep that breakdown off the **primary** anomaly email so one shelf does not drown a leadership story about a single `code` family.
(2) Alerting model: the z-score (or a similar residual) is a *programme* signal—route to a Sev3 or P4 problem ticket with an owner, not the same on-call P1 channel as 19.1.1, unless you already merged those roles by policy. Include the *worst* `code` values and a link to Cisco’s Faults Reference in the body.
(3) Cadence: weekly fleet reliability PDF or wiki export from the dashboard for the compute platform stand-up; *vendor management* takes evidence when a `code` persists across several weeks and recommended firmware is already available; the **capacity** or refresh team uses `affected_dns` and chassis splits to order capital where repeated FRU or environmental codes cluster on aging shelves.
(4) RACI (example): platform engineering owns the dashboard and the dedup design; the vendor TAM sees persistent `code` clusters with Cisco KB references; facilities sees environmental-`type` ramps; the NOC is not the first owner of this UC unless you intentionally collapse roles.

**Step 5 — Troubleshooting**
- The weekly report shows *zero* `arrivals` after a TA upgrade — the modular **faults** input is still disabled, the index in the user role is wrong, or your `where` is too strict (for example, `sev` is empty on some builds until you add another `coalesce` key you saw in the Step 1 probe). Re-run *without* the `where` in a 24-hour lab window, then re-tighten.
- You see ~2× the expected arrival line after a syslog project — **dedup** is missing or the wrong key. Compare `| stats count` with and without `dedup` on the same 60 minutes before you “tune a threshold” that only masked double ingress.
- The `chart` is blank but `stats` in the same window returns rows — a post-process command in a dashboard accidentally dropped `_time` or the visualisation is bound to a different token time range. Remove downstream `head` in the same chain and re-bind the panel.
- A single `chassis-3` swamps the stack while the fleet is fine — add a *branch* search or a `where` for `dn` to exclude that `chassis-#` and open 19.1.1 in **live** state mode for a FRU RMA, not a domain-wide *trend* crisis.
- Z-score is always near zero — `base_sd` is high because you have not enough daily buckets, or a single noisy month inflated **stdev**. Move to a **28-day** rolling `streamstats` mean or a `summary` index with a **dow** key.
- `occur` is always null in your data — your TA build does not map `occur`; the storm *panel* in your deck should fall back to `count` and you must drop language about “retrigger intensity” in executive slides to avoid a credibility gap—fix the field mapping, not the audience.
- Dramatic *severity* mix shift the day a UCSM or TA normalised case — rebuild summary accelerations, or re-run a one-off backfill with the same `lower` pipeline. Do not re-open a hardware P1 on a **measurement** inflection with no new `code`.
- UCSM GUI “active fault” count and Splunk `arrivals` disagree in the same 24h — expected: the GUI filters **open** *state*; 19.1.1 aligns with the GUI, 19.1.4 does not try to, by design. Quote both in programme reviews, not *either* in isolation.
- A cluster’s index volume drops to nothing after a migration announcement — treat as **governance** (Intersight cutover or changed VIP), not as “UCS got healthy overnight”.
- `distinct_codes` explodes in one day, `arrivals` barely moves — you likely had a *poll* that re-touched the entire MO table with low-severity *noise*; filter `severity` in a diagnostic clone before you accuse a **code** of spreading.


## SPL

```spl
index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:faults" earliest=-30d@d
| dedup dn, code, severity, _time
| eval sev=lower(coalesce(severity, sev, ""))
| eval code=coalesce(code, fault_code, "UNKNOWN")
| eval ftype=lower(coalesce(type, fault_type, ""))
| eval cause=lower(coalesce(cause, fault_cause, ""))
| where sev!="cleared" AND sev!=""
| eval sev_rank=case(sev="critical",1, sev="major",2, sev="minor",3, sev="warning",4, sev="condition",5, sev="info",6, 1=1, 99)
| bin _time span=1d
| stats count as arrivals, dc(dn) as affected_dns, dc(code) as distinct_codes, first(sev_rank) as sev_rank by _time, sev
| sort 0 sev_rank, _time
| chart useother=f sum(arrivals) over _time by sev
```

## Visualization

(1) Stacked line or area from the `chart` output: daily `arrivals` by `sev` over 30d (`span=1d`, or 1h for 7d). (2) Top-`code` table with 7d vs prior-7d delta (see detailedImplementation). (3) Two single values: `critical+major` this week vs last week, same base search. (4) `code`×week heatmap for emerging families. (5) `dc(dn)` or chassis-`rex` bar for fleet split; do not use as the only anomaly panel—shelf-local noise differs from a domain-wide `code` ramp.

## Known False Positives

**Firmware-wave bursts** after bundle activation or IOM/CIMC resets often produce a dense cluster of `equipment-degraded` and related codes across many DNs. On a 30-day stacked view this can resemble a reliability collapse, yet it is the change calendar in motion. Pair splunk results with a maintenance lookup keyed to chassis, rack, or change record before you re-tune a baseline. **Syslog and API double counting** is common when two feeds each emit a create event with slightly different `_time`. If you have not `dedup`’d, daily arrival totals read roughly double and your rolling z-score baseline shifts, hiding a genuine post-change uptick. Confirm `| stats count by source, sourcetype` and tighten dedup keys only after you see real duplicates, not the day one analyst guessed wrong. **TA version shifts in severity spelling** (Title-Case `Critical` vs `critical`) or field names split historical summary indexes if you only fixed `lower()` in a new app version. A vertical line on the chart at the upgrade day is often a *measurement* change, not a real fault explosion—rebuild summary tables with a single normalisation pass. **Using `occur` like `count`**: one row with `occur=20` and one with `occur=1` are not the same operating story; a storm panel should `sum` `coalesce(occur,occurrences,1)` while a pure arrival-rate line uses deduped row counts, as here—mixing the two in one tile misleads leadership reviews. **Hour-bound API polls vs continuous syslog** can create a visible spike at the top of the hour on mixed feeds, even when the underlying hardware is steady; widen the span, split feeds, or move anomaly logic to a daily grain. **UCSM version reclassification** of some codes between `info` and `condition` can move events between the bottom stack bands across an upgrade, which looks like a policy shift, not a hardware trend. **Decommissioned assets** whose events still sit in 30 online days inflate `affected_dns` on code panels. Filter to an allow-list from inventory or cut off at decommission time. **Clock skew and duplicate `_time`** on separate forwarders can slip rows past `dedup` and exaggerate one-day buckets—investigate `(_indextime-_time)` before you chase hardware.

## References

- [Splunk Add-on for Cisco UCS (Splunkbase 2731)](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2731)
- [Cisco UCS Faults Reference, Release 4.2 (fault codes, causes, recommended actions)](https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/td/docs/unified_computing/ucs/ts/faults/reference/4-2/UCSFaultsErrorsRef_4-2.html)
- [Integrate data with Splunk: Cisco UCS (Splunk documentation)](https://help.splunk.com/en/data-management/integrate-data-with-add-ons/splunk-supported-add-ons/splunk-supported-add-ons/cisco-ucs)
- [Cisco UCS Manager XML API Reference, Release 4.2 (faultInst class)](https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/td/docs/unified_computing/ucs/sw/api/b_UCS-XML-API-Reference_UCS-Manager-4-2/b_UCS-XML-API-Reference_UCS-Manager-4-2_chapter_0100.html)
